content.text: Knowing where objects are in three-dimensional space is one of the most fundamental problems a robot must solve before it can act safely in the world. A flat camera image collapses the world onto a 2-D grid of pixels; recovering the missing third dimension — distance from the camera to each point — is what depth estimation accomplishes. The methods range from pure geometry (stereo triangulation) to learned neural networks that hallucinate plausible structure from a single image, and from active light projection to time-of-flight ranging. Each approach makes different tradeoffs in cost, range, density, and whether the result is metrically accurate or only relatively ranked.

Depth estimation is the process of computing, for every pixel (or a sparse set of points) in a camera's field of view, the distance from the camera optical center to the corresponding 3-D surface. The output is called a

— a 2-D image where the value at each pixel encodes depth in meters (metric depth) or an ordinal depth rank (relative depth). Depth maps are the bridge between 2-D perception and 3-D reasoning: from them you can reconstruct a point cloud, build an occupancy grid, estimate ego-motion, and compute safe distances to obstacles.

The field broadly divides into passive methods, which use ambient light (monocular cameras, stereo pairs), and active methods, which illuminate the scene themselves (structured light, Time-of-Flight, LiDAR). Each class has sub-variants with distinct accuracy characteristics and hardware cost profiles.

Without depth, a robot cannot determine whether an obstacle is 30 cm away or 3 m away, making safe navigation impossible. Grasping requires knowing the pose of an object in 3-D, not just its 2-D bounding box. Autonomous vehicles need reliable depth out to 100 m at highway speeds. Indoor service robots need dense, low-latency depth at short range to avoid legs, pets, and dropped items. Every downstream module — path planning, collision avoidance, SLAM, semantic scene understanding — consumes depth information.

Depth estimation is also central to augmented reality (placing virtual objects at correct occlusion depth), 3-D reconstruction (building textured meshes), and medical robotics (intra-operative tissue distance estimation). Improvements in neural depth estimation have unlocked depth-aware features in consumer smartphones without dedicated hardware.
Enables safe collision avoidance without contact sensors by giving the planner real distances to obstacles in real time.
Allows reconstruction of 3-D point clouds from cheap RGB cameras via stereo or monocular learning, reducing sensor cost.
Complements object detection by giving 3-D bounding volumes, enabling grasp planning and manipulation.
Feeds SLAM algorithms (ORB-SLAM3, ElasticFusion) to build and maintain maps concurrently with localization.
Modern monocular networks (Depth Anything v2) run at >30 FPS on an embedded GPU, making on-robot deployment practical without an external depth sensor.
Sensor fusion combining LiDAR and monocular networks achieves accuracy exceeding either source alone in the depth-completion task.
Monocular networks produce relative depth — the scene scale is ambiguous without additional cues. A 2 m doorway and a 4 m doorway can produce identical relative maps.
Stereo SGBM fails on textureless surfaces (white walls, sky, uniform floors) where block-matching cannot find correspondences, producing noisy or invalid disparity.
Structured-light sensors (e.g., Intel RealSense D435) degrade in bright sunlight because ambient IR overpowers the projected pattern.
LiDAR is expensive ($500–$15 000), bulky, and produces sparse returns that need interpolation for dense depth maps.
ToF sensors suffer from multipath interference on shiny or transparent objects, and their range is typically limited to 10 m.
Neural monocular depth is brittle to distribution shift — a model trained on driving data may produce poor estimates on indoor scenes.
All depth sensors add latency; real-time systems must budget depth inference within the control loop tick.
The fundamental geometry behind stereo depth is triangulation. When two calibrated cameras with known baseline
observe the same 3-D point, that point appears at different horizontal positions in the two images. The difference in horizontal pixel coordinate is called
d. Given focal length f (in pixels), depth Z follows directly from the thin-lens disparity equation:

As disparity grows smaller (distant objects), depth becomes less precise and the uncertainty grows quadratically. The stereo matching problem — finding corresponding pixels between left and right images — is solved by algorithms such as
, which aggregates matching costs along multiple diagonal scan paths to regularize the disparity field globally while remaining real-time capable.
Monocular depth estimation bypasses geometry and instead learns a statistical mapping from image appearance to depth from large supervised datasets. Models like

use a Vision Transformer backbone to produce high-resolution relative depth maps. Scale ambiguity is an inherent limitation: unless the model was trained on metric-labelled data (e.g., from LiDAR supervision), the output is a relative ranking that must be scale-aligned to metric units using known object sizes or sparse sensor readings.

Active sensors sidestep the correspondence problem. A Time-of-Flight sensor emits modulated IR light and measures the phase shift of the returning wave to compute distance directly. Structured light (e.g., RealSense D400 series, Kinect) projects a known IR dot or stripe pattern and uses a single IR camera to detect the deformation of the pattern caused by scene geometry.
The diagram below shows the full depth estimation pipeline from input sensors through per-method processing to fused outputs used downstream.

attrs.alt: Depth Estimation Pipeline: input sources (monocular, stereo, ToF, LiDAR) through estimation methods to sensor fusion and output depth maps

content.text: Use stereo SGBM when you need metric depth, have a controlled indoor or structured environment, and cost is a constraint — a pair of USB cameras and calibration is sufficient.
Use a structured-light depth camera (RealSense, Azure Kinect) for indoor service robots and manipulation where robustness and ease of integration matter more than outdoor use.
Use monocular deep learning (MiDaS, Depth Anything) when you must add depth perception to a system that already has a single camera and cannot add hardware, accepting relative-only output.
Use LiDAR for outdoor autonomous vehicles and long-range navigation where metric precision and range > 50 m are required regardless of texture.
Use LiDAR + monocular depth completion when you have a LiDAR but need a dense depth map — the network fills in the gaps between sparse LiDAR returns using the RGB image as context.

The two most common implementation paths are: (1) OpenCV stereo matching for metric depth from a calibrated stereo pair, and (2) a pre-trained neural network for monocular depth from a single RGB frame. Both paths produce a depth map that can be projected to a point cloud with the camera intrinsics.
For stereo, the required calibration parameters are the camera matrices (K_left, K_right), distortion coefficients, the rotation matrix R and translation vector T between the two cameras. OpenCV's

computes these from checkerboard images. After rectification, corresponding pixels lie on the same horizontal scanline, enabling efficient block matching. SGBM uses a semi-global energy minimization over multiple 1-D scan paths.
Example 1: Stereo SGBM depth map with OpenCV

import cv2
import numpy as np

# ---- Calibration data (from cv2.stereoCalibrate) ----
# Camera matrices and distortion coefficients
K1 = np.array([[718.8, 0, 607.1],
               [0, 718.8, 185.2],
               [0, 0, 1]], dtype=np.float64)
K2 = K1.copy()   # same model camera for this example
D1 = D2 = np.zeros(5)
# Rotation and translation between cameras (baseline = 0.54 m)
R = np.eye(3)
T = np.array([[-0.54], [0.0], [0.0]])

# ---- Rectification ----
image_size = (1242, 375)   # (width, height)
R1, R2, P1, P2, Q, _, _ = cv2.stereoRectify(
    K1, D1, K2, D2, image_size, R, T,
    flags=cv2.CALIB_ZERO_DISPARITY, alpha=0
)
map1x, map1y = cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap(K1, D1, R1, P1, image_size, cv2.CV_32F)
map2x, map2y = cv2.initUndistortRectifyMap(K2, D2, R2, P2, image_size, cv2.CV_32F)

# ---- Load stereo images ----
left  = cv2.imread("left.png",  cv2.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE)
right = cv2.imread("right.png", cv2.IMREAD_GRAYSCALE)

left_rect  = cv2.remap(left,  map1x, map1y, cv2.INTER_LINEAR)
right_rect = cv2.remap(right, map2x, map2y, cv2.INTER_LINEAR)

# ---- SGBM stereo matching ----
sgbm = cv2.StereoSGBM_create(
    minDisparity=0,
    numDisparities=128,     # must be divisible by 16
    blockSize=5,
    P1=8  * 3 * 5**2,       # smoothness penalty for 1-pixel disparity change
    P2=32 * 3 * 5**2,       # smoothness penalty for >1-pixel disparity change
    disp12MaxDiff=1,
    uniquenessRatio=10,
    speckleWindowSize=100,
    speckleRange=32,
    mode=cv2.STEREO_SGBM_MODE_SGBM_3WAY
)
disp = sgbm.compute(left_rect, right_rect).astype(np.float32) / 16.0  # convert to float

# ---- Disparity → metric depth ----
# Z = f * b / d  (Q matrix handles this automatically)
points_3d = cv2.reprojectImageTo3D(disp, Q)   # shape (H, W, 3), last channel = Z
depth_map = points_3d[:, :, 2]               # metric depth in metres

# Mask invalid disparities (d <= 0)
valid_mask = disp > 0
depth_map[~valid_mask] = 0.0

print(f"Depth range: {depth_map[valid_mask].min():.2f} m  –  "
      f"{depth_map[valid_mask].max():.2f} m")

# ---- Visualise ----
disp_vis = cv2.normalize(disp, None, 0, 255, cv2.NORM_MINMAX, cv2.CV_8U)
cv2.applyColorMap(disp_vis, cv2.COLORMAP_MAGMA)
cv2.imwrite("disparity.png", cv2.applyColorMap(disp_vis, cv2.COLORMAP_MAGMA))
Example 2: Monocular depth estimation with Depth Anything v2

# pip install transformers torch torchvision pillow
from transformers import pipeline
from PIL import Image
import numpy as np
import cv2

# Load the Depth Anything v2 pipeline (downloads on first run ~400 MB)
depth_estimator = pipeline(
    task="depth-estimation",
    model="depth-anything/Depth-Anything-V2-Small-hf"
)

# ---- Inference on a single frame ----
image = Image.open("scene.jpg").convert("RGB")
result = depth_estimator(image)
# result["depth"] is a PIL Image in "I" (32-bit int) mode — relative depth
relative_depth = np.array(result["depth"], dtype=np.float32)

# Normalize to [0, 1]
d_min, d_max = relative_depth.min(), relative_depth.max()
norm_depth = (relative_depth - d_min) / (d_max - d_min + 1e-8)

# ---- Scale alignment (affine fit to sparse LiDAR or known distance) ----
# If you have sparse metric ground truth at pixels (u, v) with depths z_gt:
# solve:  z_gt = scale * norm_depth[v, u] + shift
# using numpy least-squares:
def scale_align(norm_depth, gt_pixels, gt_depths):
    A = np.column_stack([
        norm_depth[gt_pixels[:, 1], gt_pixels[:, 0]],
        np.ones(len(gt_depths))
    ])
    scale, shift = np.linalg.lstsq(A, gt_depths, rcond=None)[0]
    return scale * norm_depth + shift

# Example: single known distance (e.g., ground plane at 1.8 m at image centre)
gt_pixels = np.array([[image.width // 2, image.height // 2]])
gt_depths = np.array([1.8])
metric_depth = scale_align(norm_depth, gt_pixels, gt_depths)

# ---- Depth to point cloud ----
fx = fy = 600.0   # approximate focal length for a 640x480 image
cx, cy = image.width / 2, image.height / 2
H, W = metric_depth.shape
u, v = np.meshgrid(np.arange(W), np.arange(H))
X = (u - cx) * metric_depth / fx
Y = (v - cy) * metric_depth / fy
Z = metric_depth
points = np.stack([X, Y, Z], axis=-1).reshape(-1, 3)  # (N, 3)
print(f"Point cloud: {points.shape[0]} points")

# ---- Colourmap for display ----
vis = cv2.normalize(metric_depth, None, 0, 255, cv2.NORM_MINMAX, cv2.CV_8U)
cv2.imwrite("depth_mono.png", cv2.applyColorMap(vis, cv2.COLORMAP_INFERNO))
Example 3: LiDAR-guided depth completion (sparse to dense)

import numpy as np
from scipy.interpolate import griddata

def lidar_depth_completion_naive(sparse_depth: np.ndarray,
                                  rgb: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    A simple (non-learned) depth completion using spatial interpolation.
    For production use a learned model such as NLSPN or CompletionFormer.

    sparse_depth: (H, W) float32, 0 where no LiDAR return
    rgb:          (H, W, 3) uint8 — not used in naive version but
                  colour-guided methods weight by colour similarity
    Returns: (H, W) dense metric depth
    """
    H, W = sparse_depth.shape
    # Valid LiDAR pixels
    ys, xs = np.where(sparse_depth > 0)
    values  = sparse_depth[ys, xs]

    # Target grid
    grid_y, grid_x = np.mgrid[0:H, 0:W]

    # Interpolate using linear method (nearest/cubic also available)
    dense = griddata(
        points=(xs, ys),
        values=values,
        xi=(grid_x, grid_y),
        method="linear",
        fill_value=0.0
    ).astype(np.float32)

    # Fill remaining holes (boundary pixels) with nearest-neighbour
    holes = dense == 0.0
    if holes.any():
        dense[holes] = griddata(
            points=(xs, ys),
            values=values,
            xi=(grid_x[holes], grid_y[holes]),
            method="nearest"
        ).astype(np.float32)

    return dense

# ---- Demo ----
H, W = 375, 1242
sparse = np.zeros((H, W), dtype=np.float32)
# Simulate 5% LiDAR fill (typical 64-beam Velodyne on KITTI)
np.random.seed(0)
ys = np.random.randint(H // 2, H, size=3000)
xs = np.random.randint(0, W, size=3000)
sparse[ys, xs] = 10.0 + 30.0 * np.random.rand(3000)   # depths 10–40 m

dense = lidar_depth_completion_naive(sparse, rgb=np.zeros((H, W, 3), np.uint8))
print(f"Fill rate before: {(sparse > 0).mean() * 100:.1f}%")
print(f"Fill rate after:  {(dense  > 0).mean() * 100:.1f}%")

The KITTI Depth Completion benchmark is the canonical real-world test for LiDAR-guided depth completion. Velodyne HDL-64E LiDAR returns are projected into the camera image plane, giving a sparse depth map with roughly 5% fill. Models like

use the sparse LiDAR and the rectified camera image jointly to produce a dense metric depth map at 100% fill with RMSE below 800 mm at up to 80 m range. This dense output then feeds the vehicle's occupancy grid and collision prediction.

On the consumer side, Apple's iPhone Pro uses a LiDAR scanner (a VCSEL-based direct ToF array) to measure depth at ~1 m accuracy out to 5 m for AR placement. This is combined with the rear camera's stereo pair and software depth estimation to give a dense depth map used by the ARKit framework.
Using a monocular depth map as metric input to a collision planner without scale alignment — the robot has no idea whether an object is 1 m or 10 m away.
Skipping stereo rectification or using stale calibration — even 1 pixel of vertical misalignment doubles the minimum valid disparity range and introduces systematic depth errors.
Setting numDisparities too small in SGBM — nearby objects need large disparity values; if the range is insufficient those pixels return invalid (−16 before division).
Forgetting to filter invalid disparity pixels (value ≤ 0 after /16) before downstream use — projecting them gives spurious 3-D points at near-infinite distances.
Running a heavy ViT-based depth model (DPT-Large) at full resolution on CPU — inference takes 10+ seconds per frame, making the output stale by the time it reaches the planner.
Projecting a LiDAR point cloud into an un-extrinsically-calibrated camera image — the depth map will be spatially misaligned with the RGB, causing incorrect obstacle locations.
Always verify stereo calibration with reprojection error < 1 pixel and re-calibrate if the cameras are moved or the rig is impacted.
Apply WLS (Weighted Least Squares) post-filtering after SGBM to smooth disparity edges while preserving discontinuities at true depth boundaries.
For monocular deep models, use scale-and-shift alignment against a handful of reliable sparse reference depths (e.g., ground-plane LiDAR points) before feeding to metric-sensitive modules.
Use half-precision (FP16) inference and TensorRT or ONNX Runtime for on-device neural depth — this typically achieves 2–4x speedup versus FP32 PyTorch.
Fuse temporal frames with a Kalman filter on the depth map — this suppresses per-frame noise and fills transient holes from reflective surfaces.
Match sensor to environment: structured-light sensors in controlled indoor spaces; stereo or LiDAR for outdoor, day/night, long-range needs.

Stereo SGBM on 640×480 imagery runs at 30–60 FPS on CPU (OpenCV's optimized implementation uses SSE/NEON). On 1280×720 this drops to ~10 FPS on a single core. Neural monocular models span a wide range: Depth Anything v2-Small (ViT-S backbone) runs at 40 FPS on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin GPU; DPT-Large runs at ~5 FPS on the same hardware. LiDAR processing (projection + depth completion) adds 5–20 ms per frame on an embedded GPU for KITTI-resolution inputs (375×1242).

Memory footprint is dominated by the neural model weights (ViT-S ~90 MB, ViT-L ~300 MB) and the intermediate feature maps. At 640×480 the feature maps for a small ViT peak around 500 MB of GPU VRAM during inference. If VRAM is constrained, sliding-window inference on patches (with appropriate overlap for boundary continuity) is an option at the cost of boundary stitching artefacts.
CPU-real-time at VGA; calibration tools included
Plug-and-play structured-light depth frames as numpy arrays
Best zero-shot generalisation as of 2024; ViT-S through ViT-G variants
Multiple backbone options; DPT-Hybrid is good balance of speed vs accuracy
create_point_cloud_from_depth_image; TSDF fusion for multi-frame reconstruction
State-of-the-art on KITTI Depth Completion; requires paired RGB + sparse depth
Converts depth images to point clouds; works with RealSense and stereo cameras
Depth sensors can be attacked physically or electronically. Structured-light and ToF sensors are vulnerable to
— an adversary shining a strong IR source at the sensor can saturate its photodetectors, producing all-zeros or all-max depth outputs. LiDAR sensors can be confused by

— firing laser pulses synchronised to the sensor's rotation to inject phantom returns. Monocular networks are susceptible to adversarial patches placed in the scene that cause large depth errors at specific pixels.

Mitigation strategies include sensor redundancy (never relying solely on one modality for safety-critical decisions), plausibility checks (rejecting depth values that change discontinuously between frames faster than any physical object could move), and range sanity bounds (clipping outputs to the physical sensor range and flagging values outside it as invalid).
Depth estimation converts 2-D images into 3-D distance information — essential for collision avoidance, grasping, SLAM, and navigation.
Stereo SGBM gives metric depth with low-cost hardware but fails on textureless surfaces; always validate calibration with sub-pixel reprojection error.
Monocular deep models (Depth Anything v2, MiDaS, DPT) produce relative depth only — scale alignment against known distances is required before metric use.
Active sensors (structured light, ToF) excel indoors but degrade in sunlight; LiDAR is the most robust and accurate outdoor option at the highest cost.
Depth completion (sparse LiDAR + RGB → dense depth) outperforms either source alone and is the preferred approach on sensor-fused platforms.
Real-time deployment requires careful model selection: Depth Anything v2-Small at FP16 on a Jetson GPU achieves 40 FPS — larger models do not.
Sensor reliability requires cross-modal validation and plausibility checks; no single depth sensor should be trusted unconditionally in safety-critical paths.
